chapters.title: The wounds that shape us
chapters.text:
George always sensed he was different as a child. At that time, a small boy who preferred dolls and hairstyles over sports and cars was seen as unusual. In his daydreams, he pictured himself as a girl.

He had no idea yet that his identity would sit at the crossroads of being Black and queer, two parts of himself he had yet to understand.

Born in a small city in New Jersey, George grew up in a middle class family. His parents worked hard to give their sons a better life. Their father put in long hours as a police officer and their mother held two jobs, so the brothers were mostly raised by their grandmother, Nanny.

George remembers one story that shaped his whole childhood. A five year old could not walk home from school alone, so his cousins joined him that day. The three of them ran into a group of six strangers who attacked them. The quiet boy in the group, George, became the main target. The attackers kicked out his teeth, and he bit one attacker's leg in return.

Later, the police officer asked him to open his mouth to record the injuries. There were no counselors to help George talk through what had happened. At the time, the stereotype of a proper Black boy called for toughness, roughness, and the ability to ignore pain. So that is the mask he wore, in front of his community and in front of children who did not respect his Blackness.

Years passed, but the five year old who had stopped smiling was still inside him. George went on a journey to explore his identity and to see how ideas about manhood and Blackness had shaped it. He tells his story for the millions of queer and Black people who never had the chance to tell their own.

chapters.title: Boy or girl?
chapters.text:
The confusion about George's gender started the moment he was born. Nanny first shouted that it was a girl, then ran back a few minutes later to say it was a boy. Gender is the first label and limit placed on us as children, even when we feel and know ourselves differently. It pushes people into tidy boxes that society has built for them. In a fair world, children would not have the stress of fitting in.

Since birth, LGBTQIAP+ people have fought for the right to name themselves outside of what is considered normal.

George also struggled to fit in. He had a lot of attitude as a child, which led other kids to call him a sissy and other cruel names. He felt at ease around girls and made most of his friends at school among them. Girly habits became part of who he was until he built his own way of carrying himself.

His cool uncle's girlfriend went by Honey, so the boy invented his own nickname, Honeychild. His classmates picked it up and started calling each other Honeychild too. A parent complained that kids were using the word at home, and the school called George's mother in for a meeting. He was told to stop using the term.

When you are a child who is different, there always seems to be a something. You cannot switch, you cannot say that, you cannot act this way. There is always a part of you that has to be erased.

George saw that adults wanted to control his idea of manhood. He never forgot the word Honeychild, and he retreated into his imagination, where no one could judge him. There were bright moments, though. While on a trip with Nanny, she asked each grandchild to pick one item they wanted most. George did not hesitate. He wanted cowboy boots. Nanny asked again, but the boy was sure. Once he had them, he barely took them off, since they felt like a mix of the boy and the girl inside him.

chapters.title: Coming to terms with his Blackness
chapters.text:
George found that school played a big part in how he came to accept his identity as a Black boy. His first school was full of Black students, while most teachers were white. Older Black people held the lower paid jobs, like janitors and secretaries. George looked forward to Black history month, when he could present his Black heroes and feel proud of who he was.

The history they were taught, however, centered on white men. George admired Abraham Lincoln at first. He only learned the harder truths of history after moving to an all Black school. There, Black teachers told the real story of slavery and segregation. George finally embraced his African American history, which had been hidden behind the white American version.

Just as he came to that understanding, his parents moved him to a mostly white and Filipino Catholic school. The rules were very different, since it was a private religious school. He was now a minority, and he faced a steady stream of small slights from his classmates.

Microaggression happens when a person quietly and indirectly insults or devalues you because of your group.

George now had to answer racially loaded questions like, Is that your real hair and skin. He felt that everyone in that space kept pointing out his racial difference. The school had many rules tied to religion, and he did his best to follow them.

One episode stood out. In a history class, a teacher kept calling slavery a normal practice for the time. George did not like hearing slavery described as if it were expected, as if the teacher was justifying past wrongs rather than condemning them. When George spoke up, the teacher defended his view, saying that if he had lived back then, he would probably have owned slaves too.

The class was stunned, and George felt a flash of hot anger. How could a teacher say that to children. The teacher moved on quickly, and his failure as a moral guide was clear. Still, George felt proud that he had spoken up.

chapters.title: The family that held him
chapters.text:
As a shy child, George found comfort at home. Nanny was the family's main caregiver, the best friend, cook, and entertainer all in one. She gave herself to the family and looked after everyone. As George grew more isolated at school, Nanny was the first to notice, and she made him her partner in her small side projects. Selling things at flea markets on weekends became their favorite activity.

Nanny became George's main support as he figured out his identity. He always felt accepted by her. When he came out to her at 25, she told him he did not have to worry about embarrassing the family. Nanny saw him for who he was and loved him no matter what. She had survived breast cancer twice before doctors found a rare mass in her brain. Now it was George's turn to care for her. He never saw it as a duty, only as a way to give back to the woman who had shaped him.

Find a support system, a circle of friends or family, where you can be your true self.

His relationship with his parents mattered just as much. His father Gregory was a strong but caring presence. He had quiet ways of showing love, even if he never said so out loud. Gregory's older son from another marriage, G.G., was also gay. Gregory had struggled to build a connection with G.G. at a time when queer visibility was rare, in the 1970s, and he felt he owed it to George to do things differently. He supported George through private school and partly through college, although they stayed somewhat distant.

George was almost 21 when his father was hospitalized for heart failure. It was the first time he saw his dad scared, exposed, and vulnerable. After he recovered, his father became more open and more grateful for life.

George's mother was just as important. She set an example of staying an independent woman in a household shaped by men. When he came out to her, she told him that his sexuality would never be a disappointment to the family. She only hoped he would stay safe and make good choices.

chapters.title: First sexual experiences
chapters.text:
Sadly, not every family memory was a good one. George was about 13 when his first sexual experience happened. His older cousin started a sexual interaction with him. It felt wrong to George, because they were family, and cousins were not supposed to do those things. Afterward, he made his cousin promise to keep it a secret.

Only later did George understand that his cousin's behavior was wrong, and that he had been a victim. Even so, he felt some sympathy for him, since his cousin was a boy looking for connection in all the wrong ways and places. Even after his cousin died, George was still working through his first sexual experience as a teenager.

Seeing your identity reflected in the media and in the people around you can make you feel proud and more at ease with yourself.

It was hard for a shy Black boy like George to explore his sexuality openly. He did not see any queer role models around him until one day at school. He noticed a boy on a school bus, saw the way he carried himself, and felt an instant recognition. He had never met anyone his age like him. George worked up his courage and approached the boy, and the two became good friends.

George's crush did not go away, but he hid it behind his friendly manner. One time, Zamis finally asked him straight out, Are you gay. George denied it on the spot, even though he wanted to say yes. After graduation, they lost touch, until they ran into each other again by chance at a Pride parade four years later. They are still friends today, yet George cannot help wondering what would have happened if he had told the truth.

He had a longing to be perfect. To be right. To be in line with the norm. As much as he wanted to live openly as a gay man, he also did not want to be a disappointment.

There were many times when George wanted to come out and could not. Still, he could not escape the person he was, whether he admitted it or not. Even now, he wishes he had been bold, brave, and openly queer.

chapters.title: Finding the courage to be himself
chapters.text:
It was time for George to finish school and leave behind everything that had boxed in his identity. He was excited for a new phase, a new city, new friends, and new chances. A scholarship to a university in Virginia was supposed to change everything. Yet his expectations did not match reality. He arrived on campus and froze. A year later, he had a small group of friends, but he barely took part in university life. The shy boy from home did not just disappear.

His grades dropped. He smoked several joints a day and skipped classes. When he lost his scholarship and saw that no help was coming, he finally took charge. Getting his grades back up was not enough. He wanted a community of his own. George knew some Black students on campus were in Alpha Phi Alpha, a Black fraternity. It was exactly what he needed, a brotherhood, a way to connect with other men in a nonromantic way. He later rose to fraternity president, glad to lead and serve his community.

Joining the fraternity started him on a search for his own sense of manhood, for a way to embrace and love himself. He found more than he expected, a second family far from home. Coming out to the brothers happened slowly, as he made gay friends and discovered a whole community he had not known about.

The brothers in the fraternity accepted George for who he was, showing that manhood is not set in stone.

George was 21 when his first consensual sexual experience came about. His sex education from school, family, and university was almost nonexistent, and he had no idea how to be intimate with another man. By chance, a guy from the university approached him. Things moved quickly, leading to his first kiss and first sexual experience. His second time, though, was much more painful. He was still learning to handle his sexual life as a young queer man with no real education. He had to figure out a lot on his own and eventually shape a sexual life that worked for him.

Because of the lack of sex education and the taboos around it, queer people face a higher risk of sexually transmitted infections. The CDC estimates that half of Black men who have sex with other men will contract HIV over their lifetime.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Like George, many of us carry childhood wounds into adulthood. His young adult memoir opens with a struggle to smile, and it traces how his identity unfolded over the years. Growing up Black and gay in 1990s America, he ran into many stereotypes that boxed him in. Often, he had no words or examples around him to lean on.

His Black community expected him to be nothing but tough and masculine, yet deep inside he knew he was different. Luckily, he had a strong support system at home, with his grandmother and parents always by his side. In many other settings, George had to wear a mask in front of adults and at school. Society defined normal in narrow terms, and George struggled to fit in. The lack of sexual education did not help either. In many ways, George lived through the mistakes that many teens make in adolescence, only later in life. Still, he always knew who he was behind the mask.

When he says he is not blue, George means more than just a color tied to boys in society. He says he has no regrets about how his story turned out, despite the mistakes and pain he went through. George learned his lessons and finally embraced who he truly is, with all the layers of being a Black queer man.

Try this

No matter how you see yourself, take a cue from George's story.

Look closely at the layers of your past. What shaped you.

Find a support system or circle that will fully accept you for who you are.

Be bold and brave on your journey.

Read it till the end?
